usage_notes: 'Eins' is the standalone form. Before a noun, use 'ein/eine/ein' with the appropriate gender.
common_collocations: Nummer eins, eins zu eins, eins nach dem anderen
image_search_query: single one finger raised hand number 1
image_context: A hand with one finger raised to show the number one
pronunciation_tips: Diphthong /aɪ̯/: start with open 'ah', glide to 'ee'
memory_hook: Sounds like English 'once' without the 'c' — one = eins
common_mistakes: Using 'eins' before a noun — before a noun use 'ein Hund', 'eine Katze'

usage_notes: Used in everyday counting and measurements. On the phone 'zwo' is often substituted to avoid confusion with 'drei'.
common_collocations: zwei Personen, zwei Mal, zu zweit
image_search_query: two fingers hand peace sign number 2
image_context: A hand showing two fingers in a peace sign
pronunciation_tips: Starts with /ts/ — like English 'ts' in 'bits', then the diphthong /vaɪ̯/
memory_hook: Related to English 'twin' and 'twice' — same Germanic root
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'drei' on the phone — use 'zwo' when clarity matters

usage_notes: Cardinal number. 'Dritte' is the ordinal (third).
common_collocations: drei Mal, dritte Etage, zu dritt
image_search_query: three fingers hand number 3 counting
image_context: A hand showing three fingers to indicate the number three
example_3: Wir treffen uns um drei Uhr.
pronunciation_tips: Uvular /ʁ/ after /d/; diphthong /aɪ̯/ as in 'dry'
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'three' — both come from Proto-Germanic *þrīz
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'r' as English /r/ — use the German uvular /ʁ/

usage_notes: V in German is pronounced /f/. 'Vierte' is the ordinal (fourth).
common_collocations: vier Jahreszeiten, viermal, zu viert
image_search_query: four fingers hand number 4 count
image_context: A hand showing four fingers upright
example_3_translation: Ele tem quatro anos de idade.
pronunciation_tips: 'V' is pronounced /f/ in German: 'vier' = /fiːʁ/, not /viːʁ/
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'four'; remember 'V' sounds like 'F'
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'v' as English /v/ — always /f/ in native German words

usage_notes: Contains the umlaut ü. 'Fünfte' is the ordinal (fifth).
common_collocations: fünf Minuten, die Fünf, fünfmal
image_search_query: five fingers open hand number 5
image_context: An open hand with all five fingers extended
pronunciation_tips: ü = /ʏ/: round your lips as for 'oo' but say 'ee'; final 'nf' cluster is uncommon in English
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'five'; ü is the rounded version of 'i'
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'ü' as 'u' — it requires lip rounding

usage_notes: Base of the decimal system. 'Zehnte' is the ordinal (tenth).
common_collocations: zehn Uhr, zehn Prozent, die Zehn
image_search_query: ten fingers two hands counting number 10
image_context: Two hands with all fingers extended showing ten
pronunciation_tips: 'z' in German = /ts/; 'zehn' starts with /ts/ then long /eː/
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'ten'; 'z' is always /ts/ in German
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'z' as English /z/ — always /ts/ in German

usage_notes: Numbers 21–29 follow the pattern 'einundzwanzig' (21), 'zweiundzwanzig' (22) etc. — ones before tens.
common_collocations: zwanzig Minuten, zwanzig Jahre, zwanzig Grad
image_search_query: calendar number 20 page date twenty
image_context: A calendar page showing the number 20
pronunciation_tips: Final '-ig' is /ɪç/ in Standard German (not /ɪk/)
memory_hook: Contains 'zwei' (two) + '-zig' (tens suffix) — two-tens = twenty
common_mistakes: Pronouncing '-ig' as /ɪk/ — Standard German uses /ɪç/

usage_notes: 'Hundert' stands alone for 100. 'Das Hundert' = a group of 100. 'Einhundert' is used for precise counting.
common_collocations: hundert Prozent, einhundert, ein paar Hundert
image_search_query: one hundred coins bills counting stack money
image_context: A stack of coins representing one hundred units
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: HUN-dert; final 'rt' cluster: /ʁt/ → reduced to /ɐt/ in natural speech
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'hundred' — nearly identical
common_mistakes: Adding an article when counting — say 'hundert' not 'ein hundert' in most contexts

usage_notes: Ordinal adjective; inflects for case and gender like all German adjectives. Base: 'erst-'; add adjective endings.
common_collocations: der erste Tag, zum ersten Mal, erste Hilfe
image_search_query: finish line winner first place gold medal race
image_context: An athlete crossing the finish line in first place with a gold medal
example_1: Das ist mein erster Tag hier.
example_1_translation: Este é meu primeiro dia aqui.
example_2: Zum ersten Mal bin ich in Berlin.
example_2_translation: É a primeira vez que estou em Berlim.
example_3_translation: Os primeiros socorros são muito importantes.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on first syllable: ER-ste
memory_hook: 'erst' relates to 'erst' (first/only) — similar to English 'erst' meaning 'formerly'
common_mistakes: Not declining the adjective — 'erste', 'ersten', 'erstem' etc. depending on context

usage_notes: Ordinal of 'zwei'. Declines like a regular adjective.
common_collocations: die zweite Etage, zum zweiten Mal, der zweite Platz
image_search_query: silver medal second place podium sport race
image_context: An athlete standing on the second-place podium with a silver medal
example_3_translation: A segunda porta à esquerda, por favor.
pronunciation_tips: Starts with /tsv/ — the /ts/ cluster followed by /v/
memory_hook: Ordinal of 'zwei' (two) — same root, adds '-te' suffix
common_mistakes: Using 'zweit' without adjective ending — always add the correct ending

usage_notes: Irregular ordinal — from 'drei' but stem changes to 'dritt-'. Declines as a regular adjective.
common_collocations: die dritte Etage, zum dritten Mal, der dritte Platz
image_search_query: bronze medal third place podium sport
image_context: An athlete on the third-place podium holding a bronze medal
pronunciation_tips: Stem is 'dritt-' not 'dreit-' — irregular; stress on first syllable
memory_hook: Think 'third' in English — both share the irregular consonant shift
common_mistakes: Saying 'dreiTE' — the ordinal stem is 'dritt-', not 'dreit-'

usage_notes: Used in arithmetic. Can also appear as a standalone adjective meaning 'positive' (a plus).
common_collocations: drei plus zwei, plus Minus, das Plus
image_search_query: blackboard math addition equation plus sign chalk
image_context: A maths equation written with a plus sign on a blackboard
example_2: Plus und Minus lerne ich heute.
example_3: Das ist ein großes Plus für mich.
example_3_translation: Isso é uma grande vantagem para mim.
pronunciation_tips: Pronounced exactly as in English and Latin: /plʊs/
memory_hook: Identical to English 'plus' — direct borrowing from Latin
related_words: minus, mal, geteilt durch, die Addition
common_mistakes: Confusing 'plus' with 'und' — both work arithmetically but 'plus' is more formal

usage_notes: Used in arithmetic for subtraction. Also used colloquially for temperatures below zero: 'minus zehn Grad'.
common_collocations: zehn minus drei, minus fünf Grad, das Minus
image_search_query: blackboard subtraction minus sign equation math school
image_context: A subtraction equation with a minus sign on a blackboard
example_2: Es ist minus fünf Grad draußen.
example_2_translation: Está cinco graus negativos lá fora.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on first syllable: MI-nus; long /iː/ in first syllable
memory_hook: Identical to English 'minus' — Latin origin shared by both languages
common_mistakes: Forgetting to use 'minus' for negative temperatures — say 'minus zehn Grad'

usage_notes: In arithmetic means multiplication. Also a common particle in spoken German ('Komm mal her!' = 'Come here!'). As a noun: 'das Mal' = the time/instance.
common_collocations: drei mal drei, einmal, zweimal, das Mal
image_search_query: multiplication table math times sign school board
image_context: A multiplication table written on a school board
pronunciation_tips: Long /aː/ — 'mahl' not 'mall'; do not confuse length with the English 'mall'
memory_hook: 'Mal' as in 'mal-tiply' — the 'mal' root appears in 'multiply'
related_words: die Multiplikation, einmal, zweimal, das Mal
common_mistakes: Confusing 'mal' (multiplication) with 'Mal' (instance/time) — same word, context distinguishes

usage_notes: The standard spoken form for division in German arithmetic. 'Durch' alone can also be used colloquially: 'zehn durch zwei'.
common_collocations: zehn geteilt durch zwei, geteilt durch null
image_search_query: division math equation board fraction split halves
image_context: A division equation showing a number being split into equal halves on a board
example_1: Zehn geteilt durch zwei ist fünf.
example_1_translation: Dez dividido por dois é cinco.
example_2: Wie viel ist zwölf geteilt durch vier?
example_2_translation: Quanto é doze dividido por quatro?
example_3: Teilen ist wichtig in der Mathematik.
example_3_translation: A divisão é importante na matemática.
pronunciation_tips: 'geteilt' stress on second syllable: ge-TEILT; 'durch' ends with /ç/ or /x/
memory_hook: 'teilen' = to divide/share; 'durch' = through — divided through
related_words: teilen, plus, minus, mal, die Division
common_mistakes: Using 'dividiert durch' — though technically correct, 'geteilt durch' is standard in everyday German

usage_notes: Used when reading arithmetic equations aloud. 'Gleich' alone is colloquially used in the same position.
common_collocations: zwei plus zwei ist gleich vier, ist gleich null
image_search_query: equals sign math equation result answer board
image_context: An equals sign in an arithmetic equation on a school board
example_1: Drei plus drei ist gleich sechs.
example_1_translation: Três mais três é igual a seis.
example_2: Wie viel ist das? — Das ist gleich zehn.
example_2_translation: Quanto é isso? — Isso é igual a dez.
example_3: Eins plus eins ist gleich zwei.
example_3_translation: Um mais um é igual a dois.
pronunciation_tips: 'gleich' contains /aɪ̯/ diphthong + final /ç/ — same /ç/ as in 'ich'
memory_hook: 'gleich' relates to 'alike' — to be alike is to be equal
related_words: gleich, die Gleichung, gleichen, das Ergebnis
common_mistakes: Saying 'ist gleich zu' — the 'zu' is not needed: just 'ist gleich'

usage_notes: Used for sequential identifiers (phone numbers, house numbers, ticket numbers). Not used for abstract mathematical quantities — use 'Zahl' for that.
common_collocations: die Telefonnummer, die Hausnummer, Nummer eins
image_search_query: phone number house address number sign street
image_context: A house with a clearly visible number on the door
example_1_translation: Qual é o seu número de telefone?
example_2_translation: Estou procurando o número dez da casa.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: NUM-mer; unstressed 'er' reduces to /ɐ/
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'number' — nearly identical spelling and meaning
common_mistakes: Confusing 'Nummer' (identifier) with 'Zahl' (mathematical quantity)

usage_notes: Refers to abstract mathematical numbers and figures, unlike 'Nummer' which denotes identifiers. Also appears in 'zahlen' (to pay).
common_collocations: eine gerade Zahl, eine ungerade Zahl, in Zahlen
image_search_query: numbers written chalkboard math digits row
image_context: Various digits and numbers written on a chalkboard
example_1: Die Zahl zehn ist eine gerade Zahl.
example_1_translation: O número dez é um número par.
example_2: Ich lerne die Zahlen auf Deutsch.
example_2_translation: Estou aprendendo os números em alemão.
example_3_translation: Qual número vem depois de nove?
pronunciation_tips: 'Z' = /ts/; long /aː/; ends with /l/ — 'tsaal'
memory_hook: 'zählen' = to count; 'Zahl' = number — both from same root
related_words: zählen, die Nummer, die Ziffer, zahlen
common_mistakes: Confusing 'Zahl' (number) with 'zahlen' (to pay) — same spelling, different meaning based on capitalisation

usage_notes: Regular weak verb. 'Rechnen' is the general term for arithmetic/calculation. 'Ausrechnen' = to work out a specific result.
common_collocations: im Kopf rechnen, rechnen mit, die Rechnung
image_search_query: person calculating calculator numbers pen paper math
image_context: A person solving maths problems with a pen and paper
example_1_translation: Eu gosto de calcular de cabeça.
example_2: Kannst du das für mich rechnen?
example_2_translation: Você pode calcular isso para mim?
pronunciation_tips: 'ch' in 'rechnen' = /ç/ (palatal, as in 'ich'); unstressed final '-en' reduces to /ən/
memory_hook: 'rechnen' → 'reckon' in English — same Germanic root meaning to count/calculate
related_words: die Rechnung, der Taschenrechner, ausrechnen, die Mathematik
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'ch' as /k/ — it is /ç/ after front vowels

usage_notes: Refers to the result of addition or a total amount. 'Eine hohe Summe' = a large sum of money.
common_collocations: die Gesamtsumme, eine hohe Summe, die Summe berechnen
image_search_query: calculator total sum result addition display
image_context: A calculator displaying a total sum
example_3: Das ist eine große Summe Geld.
example_3_translation: Isso é uma grande soma de dinheiro.
pronunciation_tips: Initial 's' before a vowel = /z/ in German: 'Summe' = /ˈzʊmə/
memory_hook: Cognate with English 'sum' — identical meaning and very similar spelling
related_words: addieren, das Ergebnis, rechnen, der Betrag
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 's' as /s/ — before a vowel, 's' is voiced /z/ in German

usage_notes: Used before numbers to indicate an estimate. Very common in everyday speech when precision is not needed.
common_collocations: ungefähr zehn Minuten, ungefähr hundert Leute, so ungefähr
image_search_query: approximation estimate gauge measurement rough number arrow
image_context: An arrow pointing to an approximate value on a measurement gauge
example_3: Ich bin ungefähr zwanzig Jahre alt.
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: un-ge-FÄHR; stress on third syllable; 'ä' = /ɛː/
memory_hook: Break down: 'un' (not) + 'gefähr' (danger/close) — roughly, not precisely dangerous/close
common_mistakes: Stressing the wrong syllable — the accent falls on 'FÄHR', not 'UN'

usage_notes: Used to indicate precision. Also a very common conversational agreement word ('exactly!' / 'precisely!' / 'right!').
common_collocations: genau richtig, genau zehn, ganz genau
image_search_query: target bullseye precision accurate hit center arrow
image_context: An arrow hitting the bullseye of a target, indicating precision
pronunciation_tips: Stress on second syllable: ge-NAU; diphthong /aʊ̯/ glides from 'ah' to 'oo'
memory_hook: Think of 'genuine' — something genuine is exact and authentic
common_mistakes: Overusing 'genau' as a filler — while common in speech, it can sound excessive in writing

usage_notes: Noun form of 'halb'. 'Die Hälfte von...' = half of... Compare with the adjectival 'halb' (half a, half the).
common_collocations: die Hälfte des Weges, eine Hälfte, die erste Hälfte
image_search_query: apple cut in half two equal pieces fruit table
image_context: An apple cut cleanly in half showing two equal pieces
example_1: Die Hälfte von zehn ist fünf.
example_1_translation: A metade de dez é cinco.
example_3_translation: Me dá a metade, por favor.
pronunciation_tips: 'ä' = /ɛ/; three syllables: HÄL-fte; the 'ft' cluster ends the second syllable
memory_hook: 'Halb' means half — 'Hälfte' is just the noun form, like English 'half'
related_words: halb, teilen, das Viertel, das Drittel
common_mistakes: Confusing 'halb' (adjective: 'half a pizza') with 'die Hälfte' (noun: 'the half')

usage_notes: Used for the result of a calculation or any process. 'Das Ergebnis der Rechnung' = the result of the calculation.
common_collocations: das Ergebnis berechnen, ein gutes Ergebnis, das Endergebnis
synonyms: das Resultat, die Antwort, das Fazit
image_search_query: calculator screen result answer number display math
image_context: A calculator screen showing a numerical result
example_3: Das Ergebnis der Rechnung ist richtig.
example_3_translation: O resultado do cálculo está correto.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on second syllable: er-GEB-nis; 'b' before 'n' softens slightly
memory_hook: 'Ergeben' = to yield/result in — 'Ergebnis' is what is yielded
related_words: rechnen, ergeben, die Rechnung, das Resultat
common_mistakes: Using 'das Resultat' instead — both are correct, but 'Ergebnis' is more common in everyday German

usage_notes: Regular weak verb. Primary meaning is counting. Secondary meaning 'zählen' can mean 'to count/matter' as in 'Das zählt nicht' (that doesn't count).
common_collocations: bis zehn zählen, die Tage zählen, zählen auf
image_search_query: child counting fingers learning numbers classroom
image_context: A child counting on their fingers in a classroom setting
example_3: Eins, zwei, drei — zählen wir zusammen!
example_3_translation: Um, dois, três — vamos contar juntos!
pronunciation_tips: 'z' = /ts/; umlaut 'ä' = /ɛː/; 'zählen' = /ˈtsɛːlən/
memory_hook: Related to 'Zahl' (number) — if you have a 'Zahl' you can 'zählen' (count)
related_words: die Zahl, die Nummer, rechnen, die Anzahl
common_mistakes: Confusing 'zählen' (to count) with 'zahlen' (to pay) — different vowels but look similar
